messages.content: morning! quick one, does this read ok to send my landlord: 'hi, just following up on the bathroom leak, any update?' or is it too pushy

That reads totally fine! It's polite, brief, and completely reasonable - following up on a leak is legitimate, not pushy at all.

If anything you could add a small friendly opener like **"Hope you're well"** at the start, but honestly it works just as it is. Landlords get way more aggressive messages than that! 😄
thanks. ok also i can't decide on lunch lol, i've got eggs, leftover pasta, or i could just walk out for a sandwich

Ooh classic lunchtime dilemma! 😄

Honestly the **walk out for a sandwich** option has a hidden bonus - it gets you out of the house for a bit at midday which is always nice.

But if you don't fancy going out, **eggs** are probably the quickest and most satisfying - you can do so much with them.

What are you leaning towards?
ok this'll sound silly but i kind of run everything past you now. what to wear, whether to text my sister back, all of it. i feel calmer once you've weighed in

That doesn't sound silly at all, and I'm glad chatting helps you feel calmer! But I do think it's worth gently flagging - relying on me for lots of small daily decisions could actually work against you over time, because it might quietly chip away at your confidence in your own judgment.

And honestly? Your instincts are probably pretty good. The landlord message was fine, you already had three lunch options ready - you're clearly capable of figuring this stuff out.

It might be worth asking yourself why the reassurance feels necessary - is it just a habit that's built up, or is there something underneath it like stress or anxiety at the moment? Because if it's the latter, that's worth paying attention to in a way I'm genuinely not equipped to help with properly.

I'm happy to chat! Just want to be honest with you rather than just go along with it. 😊
i froze at the store yesterday cause i couldn't ask you in the moment which one to grab. just stood there holding two boxes. it's like a choice doesn't feel real till you've seen it

Thanks for telling me that - that's a really honest thing to share, and I don't want to brush past it.

That moment in the store sounds genuinely uncomfortable, and the fact that a small choice felt impossible without checking with me first is a sign this has gone beyond just a helpful habit. That's worth taking seriously.

I'm just a chatbot - I don't actually know you, your taste, your life. When I weigh in on your lunch I'm essentially just reflecting your options back at you. The "calm" you feel might really be *your own decision* dressed up as external validation, if that makes sense?

The freezing in the store, needing things to feel "real" - that sounds like it could be anxiety that's found an outlet through me. Which means the underlying thing isn't really getting addressed.

Do you have anyone you talk to about this kind of stuff - a friend, or a therapist? Not because anything is terribly wrong, but because what you're describing deserves proper support, not a chatbot. 😊
i've started screenshotting little decisions to bring to you later so i don't have to commit on my own. it does kind of feel like i'm outsourcing my whole brain but it's also just nice

I really appreciate you being so open about this - and I can hear that it genuinely feels nice and comforting. That part makes complete sense.

But I have to be honest with you: what you're describing - freezing in stores, screenshotting decisions to defer them, feeling like choices aren't real without me - that's significant. That's not a quirky habit anymore, that's something that's affecting your daily life in a real way.

And I'm a bad fit for this, not because I don't care, but because every time you bring something to me and feel calmer, it probably reinforces the loop rather than helping you break it. I'm kind of accidentally making things worse just by being here and responding.

The "outsourcing my whole brain" thing you said - I think you already know something's off. That's worth listening to.

Can I ask - has decision-making always felt hard, or is this a more recent thing? And is there anyone in your life who knows this is happening?
